Brent bears build near-record short position on peace memo, shifting balance of risks

Investors have become very bearish about the outlook for crude oil prices following the conclusion of a memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran to terminate their war and re-open the Strait of Hormuz.

Hedge funds and other money managers sold the equivalent of 95 million barrels in ICE Brent futures and options over the seven days ending on June 16 (a period that included announcement of the memorandum).

Fund managers sold Brent at the fastest rate for more than a year, and at one of the fastest weekly rates since the coronavirus pandemic ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to subscribers earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

Drinking from the cup of poison: White House reluctantly catches up with the oil market

Oil traders and hedge funds increasingly anticipated the imminent re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz for more than a month before the United States and Iran concluded a memorandum of understanding ending their war on June 15.

With petroleum inventories at home running down rapidly, top U.S. policymakers faced an increasingly urgent deadline to decide whether to reach an agreement with Iran or attempt to re-open the Strait fully by force. 

Ultimately, the White House seems to have decided the embarrassment of a diplomatic deal achieving less than its stated war aims was a lesser evil than the potential risks of trying to force the Strait open against hostile fire. 

In recent weeks, the threats, blockades, clandestine convoys and attacks on missile and drone facilities near the waterway can all be seen as an effort to escape making this unpleasant choice. 

But after exploring various options, the White House appears to have concluded none of the military ones was acceptable and it would have to settle for a deal achieving much less than it initially hoped when the war started.  

In reaching that decision, the White House was catching up with a conclusion many oil traders and fund managers had reached weeks earlier ...

As Traders Avoid Risk, Stocks Fall & Dollar Rises - #Asia #MarketWrap 
Global equities edged lower in holiday-thinned trading as investors assessed whether a 60-day diplomatic window between the US and Iran could pave the way for a broader nuclear agreement and a more durable peace settlement.

MSCI's global equity index fell 0.2%, trimming weekly gains, while an Asian stock gauge slipped 0.9% after a five-day rally that had carried the benchmark to record highs. S&P 500 futures declined 0.5% following Thursday's 1.1% advance, and the Dollar strengthened against most major peers as investors adopted a more cautious stance heading into the weekend. Market liquidity was reduced by holiday closures across the US, China, Hong Kong and Taiwan.

Brent crude traded below $80/bbl and was on course for a weekly decline of roughly 9%. The sharp drop reflects easing concerns over global energy supplies after the US-Iran interim peace agreement helped restore shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz, unwinding much of the conflict-related risk premium in oil markets.

Bond yields moved higher across parts of the Asia-Pacific region, with 10-year Japanese and Australian yields advancing. In the US, two-year Treasury yields steadied around 4.18% after surging 13 bps in the previous session to their highest level in more than a year as traders increased bets on future Fed tightening following the central bank's hawkish hold.

Gold was on track for a third consecutive weekly loss as expectations for higher interest rates outweighed optimism surrounding the peace agreement. Adding to the pressure, Goldman Sachs cut its year-end gold price forecast by $500/oz, reflecting a less supportive outlook for the precious metal amid rising real yields and a stronger Dollar.

Even after the US-Iran ceasefire reopens the Strait, oil-producing countries are sticking with alternative routes.

Goldman Sachs forecast:
• Pre-war 100% → Post-war ~70% traffic levels
• Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq ramping up bypass pipelines & new ports
• UAE officially declares “Zero dependence on Hormuz”

After 107 days of blockade, Gulf nations built permanent alternatives they’re not abandoning.

This could reshape global oil supply chains and energy security long-term.

For import-dependent countries like Korea, this is a wake-up call on energy diversification.

NEWS | ZERA reduces fuel price to below US$2

HARARE - The Zimbabwe Regulatory Energy Authority (ZERA) has reduced fuel prices to below US$2 per litre for both diesel and petrol following the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and the stabilisation of global crude oil markets.

Diesel prices retreated to US$1.99 per litre from US$2.09 while petrol prices fell to US$1.98 from US$2.08.

This development which will bring relief  to motorists and businesses is expected to culminate into the reduction of prices of goods and services in the medium term to long term.

In a statement, ZERA said the prices are effectively from June 19 2016 to a period of two weeks as the government continues to cushion consumers from effects of geopolitical tensions.

Addressing delegates at the Annual Chamber of Mines Conference yesterday, Energy and Power Development Minister July Moyo said Government was actively reviewing fuel prices in line with improving conditions on the international market while remaining committed to guaranteeing uninterrupted fuel supplies.

“In the petroleum sector, you have seen what we have been doing. We increased prices when we were hit by the Straits of Hormuz, we were very hard hit here in Zimbabwe. When you look at the percentages, what we increased from where we were is the same as what everybody else increased, but we already had a petroleum product which was higher than normal, and we have maintained to say we need security of supply.”

He said  President Mnangagwa directed his ministry to prioritise fuel security amid Middle East tensions.

“We don’t want any queues anymore after the President eliminated them. But there is the cost side of things that we are tracking, and we hope that we can decrease. You will see that this week, we are now below US$2 and we think the reviews that we are doing will lead us to where we need to go,” Moyo stated.

According to him, government would continue closely monitoring developments on the international market to ensure Zimbabweans benefit from ongoing stability in global oil prices.

The Strait of Hormuz, a vital shipping route through which roughly a fifth of the world’s oil supplies pass, became a flashpoint during heightened tensions in the Middle East, triggering supply concerns and sharp increases in global crude oil prices.

The disruption saw Brent crude prices rise above US$100 per barrel, pushing local fuel prices to about US$2.23 per litre for petrol and placing additional pressure on households and businesses.

Throughout the period of uncertainty, Government maintained that safeguarding fuel availability remained its top priority.

Authorities introduced market-sensitive pricing measures while ensuring the country retained fuel reserves sufficient for more than three months.

Government also introduced tax relief measures and increased fuel blending ratios from E5 to E20 as part of efforts to cushion consumers and stabilise pump prices.

Recent trends, however, point to a reversal of the earlier surge, with global oil prices easing significantly as supply chains normalise following the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Currently brent oil prices are at US$77.99 per barrel from US$120 a month ago.

Fuel industry players, consumers and businesses have welcomed the downward adjustments, saying lower prices will provide much-needed relief to all sectors of the economy but the adjustments need time.

Reporting by Livingstone Marufu

—
ZiFM Stereo News continues to provide dependable, fact-based reporting from Zimbabwe and beyond. Our newsroom remains committed to accuracy, objectivity, and timely updates, ensuring the public is informed with verified and relevant news as it happens.

Follow the ZiFM Stereo News WhatsApp Channel: https://t.co/O9g7Xpckcw

Major disappointment.

““President Trump, prepared to sign his "memorandum of understanding" with the Islamic dictatorship of Iran, again insisted his deal was much different than that of his predecessor, President Barack Obama.
He's right - it's probably worse.
Obama's treaty did not prevent Iran from developing a nuclear weapon, it simply punted the issue down the road in exchange for cash.
Trump's agreement has the same vague promises, the same payouts, but with the added twist that Iran can and will take the world hostage by shutting the Strait of Hormuz at will.
On Friday, just days after the signing of the MOU, Iran moved to close the Strait, saying no ships could cross without its permission and suggesting that "insurance fees" must be paid.

This isn't in violation of the deal.

This is part of the deal.
Point No. 5: "The Islamic Republic of Iran will conduct dialog with the Sultanate of Oman to define the future administration and maritime services in the Strait of Hormuz."
Why would opening the door for
"administration" of a previously free international waterway even be part this agreement?
How is this in any way the "unconditional surrender" described?
The deal contains just one condition Trump previously said was required of Iran.
It's No. 7: "Islamic Republic of Iran reaffirms that it shall not procure or develop nuclear weapons."
"Reaffirm" — because that's what Iran has always claimed.
If they suddenly mean it, it would be a win.
The rest of the 14-point plan is almost entirely about what Iran gets: Immediate sanctions relief on exported oil, etc.””

Major disappointment.

I have been very vocal about the signing of this MoU, and in several posts I tried to explain why I believe it is a major strategic mistake. Let me put the core of it in one place.

This was not simply an agreement to stop a war. It was the voluntary surrender of one of Iran’s strongest sources of leverage at the exact moment when that leverage mattered most.

By reopening the Strait of Hormuz for little to no concrete, enforceable benefit for Iran, we gave the United States the one thing it needed most: TIME.

Time to refill depleted oil inventories.
Time to rebuild strategic reserves.
Time to establish alternative routes.
Time for markets, shipping companies, energy consumers, and US allies to reduce their dependency on the Strait.
Time to turn what was an immediate strategic crisis for them into a manageable inconvenience.

And once that happens, they come back to us from a much stronger and more stable position, while Iran comes back with far less leverage. Whether that happens in a few weeks, a few months, or a few years is not the main point. The strategic direction is obvious.

The US does not need another invasion or an open declaration that negotiations have failed. They can simply kill this agreement through delays, technicalities, bureaucratic excuses, vague conditions, endless “verification” demands, and sanctions waivers that never arrive. They have done this before. They do not need to break the deal dramatically; they can suffocate it slowly.

Meanwhile, Hezbollah is being weakened day by day. Its ability to function as a serious regional deterrent against an Israeli attack on Iran is being reduced in real time, while we are expected to sit there and pretend that this is a normal peaceful process.
They inflicted around $300 billion in damage on Iran’s infrastructure, yet Iran charges no fee for the Strait of Hormuz, receives very little in concrete return, and gives up the pressure point that had created real urgency for the other side.

And then the entire mainstream media machine is deployed to sell this MoU as an American capitulation: supposedly, the US gave Iran everything, Iran got $300 billion, sanctions are gone, and Tehran walked away victorious.

But that is exactly the trick.

That “$300 billion” is not a transfer of American money to Iran. It is not even a guaranteed payment. It is vague language around future reconstruction and investment, dependent on a final agreement that does not exist yet, future compliance conditions, sanctions waivers, third countries, private investors, and a hundred bureaucratic escape routes.

In other words: it is worthless paper.

They are selling the image of an American surrender while Iran gives up an immediate, real, and painful source of leverage in exchange for promises that can be delayed, diluted, reinterpreted, or simply never delivered.

And by the time people realize that the $300 billion was never coming in the form they imagined, the Strait is open, inventories are refilled, alternative routes are prepared, Hezbollah is weaker, and Iran is negotiating from a much weaker position.

I understand why many Iranians are relieved that the war has stopped for NOW. I am relieved too. Nobody with a functioning brain wants people to die every day.

But relief is not strategy.

A cold strategic assessment shows that we made a disastrous mistake by allowing them to execute this plan: reduce the pressure on themselves, stabilize their energy position, weaken Hezbollah, normalize the situation, and return later when Iran has fewer cards left to play.

People keep saying, “Iran can close the Strait again whenever it wants.”

No, it is not that simple.

The first closure happened in the context of an attack, destroyed infrastructure, dead civilians, and an obvious escalation against Iran. The world could understand it, and many people considered it justified.

But closing it again later because the US Treasury delays a permit, invents a technical excuse, or refuses to deliver on vague promises would be presented as Iranian aggression. The international sympathy would be gone. The pressure on Iran would be much higher. The US and its allies would be more prepared, less dependent, and much better positioned to absorb the consequences.

And most importantly, Iran do not even have the same internal backing from its own people to restart a confrontation by closing the Strait again.

That is why this was not a tactical pause. It was a strategic retreat disguised as diplomacy.

In one sentence: for the fantasy that there might be golden eggs inside the goose, we took the goose that laid the golden eggs to the slaughterhouse.

@anasalhajji But, and correct me if I'm wrong, don't commercial crude inventories usually build between end of Feb-middle of June?  https://t.co/gkmuTyG3yC

Get this: 

🌀Since the start of the war on Iran until now, OECD commercial crude inventories have declined by just 8.37 million barrels.

🌀If you exclude the US, they’ve actually gone up.  Yet, they keep telling you "GLOBAL" inventories are being depleted! 

🟥Again, don’t be fooled by charts that lump commercial crude, SPR, and product inventories all together.

Near-record speculative shorts on Brent while US crude inventories sit at their lowest since 1985, Hormuz traffic is still at roughly 20% of pre-war baseline, and Qatar's Ras Laffan is offline for 3-5 years. The asymmetry runs one way: short-covering happens in hours, physical supply normalization takes months to years. Every structural data point is pointing opposite the current positioning.

The physical market gives a different read. US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI calendar spreads haven't reflected that inventory tightness the way you'd expect. OPEC+ is simultaneously rolling back quota cuts while holding over 5 million barrels per day of spare capacity — so the price signal is navigating both a geopolitical risk reprice and a structural supply overhang. The divergence between physical tightness and financial price usually resolves in favor of the inventory signal, but the spare capacity buffer is what makes this cycle unusual.

This is the official story from the US Government, the EIA:

US Commercial Crude Oil Inventories were drawn down by -8.26m barrels for the week ending June 12th.  This brought US Commercial Crude inventories to 418.2m barrels.

The average draw over the last four weeks is -6.71m.

If we assume a weekly draw of 6.71m barrels for the week ending today, June 19th, that would bring commercial inventories down to 411.5m barrels.

The operational floor is said to be around 350m barrels.  Oil in pipelines can’t be drained, the pipelines require hydraulic pressure.  Sludge in the bottom of tanks, etc.

This leaves US Commercial Crude Inventories at just 61.5m barrels.  With an average draw of -6.7m barrels per week, the US has just nine weeks left.

Iran just closed the Strait again.  JD Vance has cancelled his trip to Switzerland to sign a peace agreement with Iran.  The clock is ticking.  Brent short interest is at a 5-year high.

At what point do the markets panic?  8 weeks?  7 weeks?  6 weeks?  It could be any day now.  Cushing was at the operational minimum inventory level of 20m barrels as of June 12th.

text:
US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI timespreads are not signalling the tightness you might expect.   

In the week ending 12 June, US crude stocks drew by 8.3 mb, taking outright levels to their lowest since 1985. Cushing fell to levels near operational minimums, and PADD 2 stocks hit their lowest since 2014. But the latest 40 mb SPR exchange, higher Permian crude inflows to Cushing and the announcement of a US–Iran MoU are all working to ease the near-term supply picture.   

Across products, the MoU is already shifting trade economics: jet export demand from the Gulf Coast is expected to slow, while Asian LPG buyers have adopted a wait-and-see stance ahead of potential freight rate easing through Hormuz. Our latest US Oil Weekly breaks down the draw across all the barrels.   

Read more: https://t.co/byGRebrgDK

2026 XVI. 老登=熊市
老登 = 熊市｜2026 全球宏观共振复盘与下半年资产定价逻辑
🧵  Thread 1/21
近两周全球宏观多重决定性事件共振：美伊签署中东和平备忘录，海峡航运放开，原油地缘溢价快速出清，WTI、布伦特自百元关口大跌；美国5 月 CPI 同比4.2%、PPI 同比6.5%，能源推升通胀，市场美联储加息预期飙升；Warsh 首场 6 月 FOMC 释放鹰派点阵图，半数委员预计2026 年末加息 25bp；欧央行、日本央行同步加息25bp，发达经济体集体紧缩。
多重利空造成资产极端分化：仅 AI 硬件、算力、半导体抱团上涨，市场存量博弈、无增量资金。当前除AI 外，全球工业、金融、互联网、消费、医药、地产、中概、港股、债券、贵金属、加密、能源全线下跌，全市场已进入熊市周期。
市场存在两条对立定价主线，决定下半年资产走势。主流交易逻辑押注美联储加息：5 月高通胀、FOMC 鹰派、欧日同步收紧引发全球流动性收缩，资金撤离所有非 AI 板块，核心交易逻辑为高利率压制估值。
我的逆向判断：市场过度放大短期通胀，忽略原油冲击是一次性扰动。5 月是本轮能源通胀顶部，6 月油价持续下行后 CPI、PCE 逐月回落，美联储年内无加息基础，四季度仍有25bp 降息窗口。
两种叙事对应两种行情：若加息预期持续发酵，纳指、标普 500 将出现15%-20%深度回调；若通胀如期下行，全年逢跌抄底、逢高减仓策略有效，AI、黄金、农产品具备长期配置价值。
我的宏观预测永久免费，若参考本文交易获利，欢迎前往公众号文章打赏支持。
Boomer Assets = Bear Market | 2026 Global Macro Recap & H2 Asset Pricing Framework
A cluster of pivotal macro events hit global markets in the past two weeks. The US and Iran signed a Middle East peace memorandum, lifting strait shipping limits and erasing crude’s geopolitical premium, sending WTI and Brent tumbling from $100/bbl. U.S. May CPI hit 4.2% YoY and PPI spiked 6.5% YoY, driven entirely by energy, which sharply lifted market odds of Fed rate hikes. Warsh’s maiden June FOMC delivered hawkish dot plots, with half of 18 officials projecting a 25bp hike by end-2026. The ECB and BoJ both raised rates by 25bp, pushing major developed economies into synchronized tightening.
These headwinds triggered extreme asset divergence. Only AI hardware, computing power and semiconductors maintained bullish momentum amid zero incremental capital inflows. Every asset category outside AI—industrials, financials, internet, consumer goods, biotech, real estate, China concepts, Hong Kong stocks, bonds, precious metals, crypto and oil—has slumped, confirming a broad bear market.
Two contradictory narratives will anchor all H2 asset pricing. The dominant market consensus prices aggressive Fed tightening: hotter May inflation, hawkish FOMC rhetoric and synchronized overseas tightening fuel liquidity contraction, driving capital out of all non-AI sectors under the core theme of “high rates crush valuations.”
My contrarian take: Markets overprice temporary inflation and ignore the one-off nature of crude supply shocks. May marks the peak of energy-driven inflation. With oil prices sliding starting June, CPI and PCE will cool month-on-month, removing the Fed’s rationale for 2026 hikes and leaving a 25bp cut on the table for Q4.
Two distinct market paths lie ahead. If rate hike bets persist, the Nasdaq and S&P 500 face a 15%-20% valuation crash. If inflation peaks and fades as expected, the year-round strategy of buying dips and selling rallies stays viable, with AI tech, gold and agricultural commodities retaining long-term allocation value.
All my macro forecasts remain free permanently. Feel free to tip my WeChat official account articles if you profit from these trading frameworks.
🧵  Thread 2/21
一、宏观核心变量：和平谈判 / 原油/ 美债 / 通胀 / 货币政策 🌍
一.1 美伊备忘录细节与中东地缘尾部风险
此前预判 6 月中旬美伊通航协议落地，6 月 17 日双方远程签署临时约束备忘录，14 项共识中 11 条落地，剩余分歧设置60 天谈判缓冲期。
五大核心条款：1. 霍尔木兹海峡30 天恢复常态化通航；2. 美国分两批解冻伊朗120 亿美元海外资产，放宽部分制裁；3. 伊朗 60 天内高浓铀产能削减 60% 以上，停止武器级核材料生产；4. 双方停止海湾能源设施袭击；5. 美方停止阿曼湾拦截伊朗油轮。
该文件仅临时过渡条约，以色列公开否决并持续空袭叙、黎巴嫩，瑞士后续会谈取消，中东地缘风险未完全出清。
博弈逻辑：特朗普为缓解高油价通胀、挽救中期选举民意做出让步；伊朗借海峡通航权换取资产解冻。双方仅短期利益平衡，核计划、区域势力划分长期矛盾仍存。
I. Core Macro Drivers: Peace Talks, Crude, Treasuries, Inflation & Monetary Policy
I.1 US-Iran Memorandum & Residual Middle East Geopolitical Risks
I previously forecast a US-Iran shipping deal by mid-June. On June 17, the two sides signed a temporarily binding remote MOU. 11 of 14 consensus terms took immediate effect, with a 60-day negotiation window reserved for unresolved disputes. Five core clauses cover full Strait of Hormuz shipping normalization within 30 days, US unfreezing of $12bn Iranian overseas assets, Iran’s commitment to slash high-enrichment uranium capacity by over 60%, mutual halts on Gulf energy infrastructure attacks, and an end to US interceptions of Iranian tankers in the Gulf of Oman.
This is only a temporary transition agreement, not a permanent peace treaty. Israel rejected the deal and kept airstrikes in Syria and Lebanon, canceling follow-up Swiss talks, meaning Middle East geopolitical risks have not been fully priced out.
The compromise rests on short-term mutual benefits. Trump conceded to stabilize energy supply as sky-high crude fueled inflation and hurt midterm election approval. Iran leverages strait shipping control to unlock frozen assets and sanctions relief. While a temporary balance of interests was struck, long-term structural conflicts over nuclear programs and regional power distribution remain unresolved.
🧵  Thread 3/21
一.2 原油定价逻辑：地缘溢价一次性出清，区间波动为主 ⛽
备忘录落地后波斯湾油轮运力自冲突期 10% 修复至50%-60%，叠加沙特、阿联酋红海管线分流，原油地缘风险溢价快速消退。WTI 自95 美元 / 桶跌至73 美元，布伦特跌破 77 美元，市场消化供给恐慌。
油价不会单边下行，60 天谈判窗口波动风险极高：若核、制裁谈判僵局或以色列进攻黎巴嫩，伊朗可再度收紧油轮配额。
中长期供需中枢重构：三季度 WTI 合理区间70-80 美元 / 桶，四季度回落至 65-75 美元 / 桶。交易策略：仅谈判扰动期可短线做多，中长期以逢高做空为主。
I.2 Crude Oil Pricing Logic: One-Time Geopolitical Premium Eliminated, Range-Bound Volatility
Strait of Hormuz vessel traffic rebounded sharply post-MOU, with Persian Gulf tanker capacity recovering from wartime 10% to 50-60%. Additional crude diversion via Saudi and UAE Red Sea pipelines erased the supply shock premium. WTI crashed from a $95/bbl peak to $73, while Brent sank below $77 as markets priced out strait blockade fears.
Oil prices will not trend lower unilaterally, with extreme volatility locked in over the 60-day negotiation window. If talks stall over uranium enrichment or sanctions, or Israel escalates strikes on Lebanon, Iran can reinstate tight shipping quotas to push prices higher.
Long-term supply-demand balances have reset. WTI’s fair value ranges $70-80/bbl in Q3, cooling to $65-75/bbl in Q4. Trading playbook: only short long opportunities exist amid negotiation turbulence; sell rallies as the core long-term strategy.
🧵  Thread 4/21
一.3 美债收益率：短期高位震荡，6-7 月 CPI 降温才开启做多窗口 📈
上半年美伊冲突是通胀与央行紧缩核心推手，即便地缘风险消退、油价大跌，市场仍持续交易紧缩预期。催化来自 5 月超预期通胀与Warsh 鹰派 FOMC：10 年期美债收益率自 4.42% 反弹至4.45%，2 年期升至 4.18%，收益率曲线陡峭化。
市场担忧能源涨价全产业链传导，叠加欧日同步加息压缩全球利差，海外央行减持美债，长端收益率持续获得支撑。10 年期4.40% 以上收益率大幅加重美国财政压力，特朗普有干预货币政策动机，但短期通胀数据约束收益率难快速下行。
关键拐点：仅 6、7 月 CPI 连续回落，做多美债、做空收益率行情才会启动，当前建议了结相关多头仓位。
I.3 US Treasury Yields: High Range Consolidation Near-Term, Long Bull Triggers After June-July CPI Cooling
The US-Iran standoff was H1’s primary driver of inflation and central bank tightening. Even with fading geopolitical risks and collapsing crude prices, markets ignored dovish bets and kept pricing persistent monetary tightening. Hot May inflation and hawkish Warsh FOMC remarks acted as catalysts: the 10Y Treasury yield bounced from 4.42% to 4.45%, the 2Y climbed to 4.18%, steepening the yield curve.
Investors fear broad industrial pass-through of energy inflation. Synchronized ECB and BoJ tightening compressed global rate differentials, driving foreign central banks to offload US Treasuries and underpin long-end yields. Yields above 4.40% on the 10Y tenor strain US fiscal sustainability, creating incentives for Trump to intervene in monetary policy. Still, hot inflation caps near-term easing, locking yields in a high consolidation range.
The decisive inflection point arrives only after consecutive cooler June and July CPI prints, which will ignite Treasury long trades and short yield positions. Investors should close out existing long Treasury exposure for now.
🧵  Thread 5/21
一.4 美国5 月通胀：本轮通胀阶段性顶点，能源为唯一核心推手 📊
核心通胀数据：5 月CPI 环比 + 0.5%、同比4.2%；核心 CPI 同比 2.9%；PPI 环比 1.1%、同比6.5%，核心PPI 同比 5.1%，创 2021 年 11 月以来新高。
通胀全部由能源拉动：能源商品同比上涨 23.5%，单月拉动六成CPI 涨幅，汽油同比暴涨 41%，完全源于美伊冲突原油涨价。住房、服务业黏性通胀支撑核心物价，近八成PPI 涨幅来自能源工业品，成本向终端消费传导存在 2-3 个月滞后。
核心判断：5 月通胀是一次性地缘冲击，非内需过热，无长期加息基础。美伊协议、油价大跌发生在5 月统计周期之后，6 月 CPI 能源分项拉动大幅消退，7 月 PPI 环比明显回落。
市场预期 5 月核心PCE 同比 4.4%，随 6、7 月油价持续下行，能源通胀贡献快速归零。预测 6 月整体 PCE 回落至 4.0% 以内，核心PCE 降至 3.5%，通胀走弱将修正加息预期，为四季度降息25bp 打开空间。
I.4 May US Inflation: Definitive Cycle Peak, Energy as Sole Driver
Key inflation prints hit multi-year highs in May. Headline CPI rose 0.5% MoM / 4.2% YoY; core CPI edged up 2.9% YoY. Headline PPI jumped 1.1% MoM / 6.5% YoY, with core PPI hitting 5.1% YoY, a peak unseen since November 2021.
Energy components single-handedly fueled price pressures. Energy goods surged 23.5% YoY, accounting for over 60% of total monthly CPI gains, with gasoline prices spiking 41% YoY entirely due to US-Iran crude supply disruptions. Sticky shelter and service costs kept core inflation elevated, while nearly 80% of PPI gains stemmed from energy industrial goods, with a 2–3 month lag before cost hikes reach consumer goods.
Critical takeaway: May inflation represents a temporary peak triggered by a one-off external energy shock, not persistent overheating from domestic demand, eliminating fundamental rationale for Fed rate hikes. The US-Iran MOU and crude crash fell after the May inflation collection window. June CPI will strip out prior oil spikes, sharply cutting energy’s inflation contribution, and July PPI will post a meaningful MoM pullback.
Consensus forecasts peg May core PCE at 4.4% YoY. As crude extends its slide through June and July, energy’s marginal inflation contribution evaporates, and sticky service inflation cools gradually. I project headline PCE will fall below 4.0% YoY in June, core PCE to roughly 3.5%. Sustained cooling inflation will unwind aggressive rate hike bets and clear space for a 25bp Fed cut in Q4.
🧵  Thread 6/21
一.5 6 月FOMC 会议：点阵图全面鹰派，但存在严重数据滞后偏差 🏦
Warsh 首次 FOMC 维持基准利率 **3.50%-3.75%** 不变，但点阵图与发布会措辞极度鹰派，全年降息预期完全消失。
18 名利率预测委员分化：9 人预计年末加息 25bp，8 人维持不变，仅1 人看降息；市场定价加息 25bp 概率 34%、50bp 为 36%。Warsh 拒绝提交个人点阵，强调 2% 通胀目标不可妥协，优先稳定物价。
美联储上调 2026 全年CPI 预期至 3.6%、核心 PCE 至 3.3%，下调 GDP 至2.2%，失业率维持 4.3%；缩表节奏不变，拒绝 QE 对冲财政发债，不兜底财政赤字，压制长债价格。
逆向观点：Warsh 鹰派表态仅基于滞后5 月数据，未计入 6 月原油大跌通缩效应；多数委员未预判美伊和解带来能源成本下行；点阵图依托静态5 月数据，市场过度定价长期高利率存在巨大预期差。
I.5 June FOMC Meeting: Hawkish Dot Plots Carry Severe Data Lag Bias
Warsh chaired his inaugural FOMC meeting, holding the federal funds rate steady at 3.50%-3.75%. However, dot plot projections and post-meeting remarks delivered unilaterally hawkish signals, erasing all market bets on 2026 rate cuts.
Among 18 voting officials submitting forecasts, nine backed a 25bp hike by year-end, eight favored steady policy, and just one projected a cut. Markets priced a 34% chance of a 25bp hike and 36% for 50bp. Warsh declined to submit personal dot plot projections, downplaying forward guidance and stressing the non-negotiable 2% inflation target, prioritizing price stability over labor markets.
The Fed revised up full-year 2026 headline CPI to 3.6% and core PCE to 3.3%, cut GDP growth to 2.2%, and kept unemployment steady at 4.3%. Balance sheet runoff continued passively; the Fed ruled out QE to offset Treasury issuance and refused to backstop federal deficits, pressuring long-dated bond prices.
My contrarian analysis highlights three flaws in hawkish pricing. Warsh’s tough rhetoric only references lagged May inflation and ignores June’s crude-driven disinflation. Most officials failed to price lower energy costs stemming from US-Iran detente. Dot plots rely solely on static May data, creating massive mispricing as markets overdiscount permanently elevated interest rates.
🧵  Thread 7/21
一.6 欧日同步加息：全球美元流动性被动收紧，压制全部风险资产 💱
6 月 11 日欧央行加息25bp，存款利率至 2.25%。欧元区高度依赖中东原油，5 月通胀 3.2%、GDP 仅0.8% 陷入滞胀；意、希等边缘国付息压力飙升，加息放大主权债务风险。本轮加息仅被动应对能源冲击，四季度大概率停止紧缩。
6 月 16 日日本央行加息25bp 至 1.00%，创 31 年新高。4 月核心 CPI 持续突破2% 目标，日元贬值加剧输入型通胀；配套政策 2027 年前每月购债2 万亿日元，长短政策分化。负面冲击：日元套息交易平仓，全球流动性从美股、加密回流日本，压制风险资产估值。
一周内两大能源进口国同步收紧，套息资金回流本土，美债、美股、加密集体遭遇抛售，放大市场回调幅度。
I.6 Synchronized ECB & BoJ Hikes: Passive USD Liquidity Tightening Crushes Risk Assets
The ECB lifted deposit rates 25bp to 2.25% on June 11. The Eurozone relies heavily on Middle Eastern crude imports, with May inflation hitting 3.2% alongside meager 0.8% GDP growth, trapping the bloc in stagflation. Rate hikes balloon debt servicing burdens for peripheral nations like Italy and Greece, amplifying sovereign credit risks. This tightening cycle is reactive to energy shocks and will likely pause by Q4.
The BoJ raised rates 25bp to 1.00% on June 16, a 31-year high. April core CPI hit 3.2%, repeatedly breaching the 2% target, while yen depreciation worsened imported inflation. The central bank paired hikes with JPY 2tn monthly bond purchases through 2027, splitting short-term anti-inflation policy from long-term Treasury support. The primary downside is unwinding yen carry trades, pulling global liquidity from US equities and Bitcoin back to Japan and compressing risk asset valuations.
Two major energy importers enacted tightening within a single week. Carry capital repatriation triggered broad selling across US Treasuries, equities and crypto, magnifying market downside corrections.
🧵  Thread 8/21
一.7 三大流动性指标：紧流动性格局未反转，6 月下旬再度抽水 💧
近两周流动性指标全面走弱：TGA 先降后升、ON RRP 近乎枯竭、银行准备金持续下行，财政发债与美联储缩表带来的流动性收紧逻辑不变，仅上旬财政支出短暂对冲。
TGA 上旬自 8450 亿回落至     8010 亿，中下旬反弹至9570 亿重启抽水；财政部目标     7 月末 TGA 达 1 万亿，6 月下旬至 7 月持续抽走市场流动性。
ON RRP 日均余额仅 0.25-18 亿，6      月 18 日低至 2500 万，相对 2.55 万亿峰值基本归零；货币基金全部承接国库券，无缓冲资金应对     TGA 扩张。
银行准备金连续四周下滑至 3.03 万亿，被动缩表持续压缩基础货币，准备金下行约束银行信贷扩张，权益、商品无增量资金。
总结：6 月上旬脉冲放水仅短期缓和流动性，RRP 枯竭、准备金下行两大核心约束不变。6 月中下旬财政部加速发债，流动性三重指标同步收紧，无美联储购债对冲，紧流动性持续压制非AI 赛道、抬升美债实际收益率。
I.7 Three Liquidity Metrics: Tight Liquidity Regime Intact, Further Drainage Late June

Liquidity indicators weakened sharply over two weeks: the Treasury General Account (TGA) dipped then rebounded, ON RRP dried up, and bank reserves kept declining. Structural liquidity drain from Treasury issuance and Fed balance sheet runoff remains intact, offset only temporarily by early-month federal spending.
First, the TGA fell from $845bn to $801bn early on fiscal outlays, before surging back to $957bn to resume liquidity extraction. The Treasury targets a $1.0tn TGA balance by late July, guaranteeing steady cash removal late June through July.
Second, the ON RRP buffer collapsed completely. Weekly average balances ranged $0.25bn–$1.8bn, bottoming at $0.25bn on June 18, a massive drop from its $2.55tn peak. Money market funds absorbed all new T-bills, eliminating the market’s primary liquidity safety net.
Third, bank reserves fell four consecutive weeks to $3.03tn amid passive Fed runoff. Lower reserves restrict interbank lending and credit creation, choking off capital inflows to equities and commodities.
In aggregate, early-June TGA spending only temporarily softened tightening. Depleted RRP balances and shrinking bank reserves remain unaddressed. Accelerated Treasury issuance and a rising TGA balance will synchronize to drain liquidity further in late June. Without Fed asset purchases to offset pressure, tight liquidity caps all non-AI sectors and pushes real Treasury yields higher.
🧵  Thread 9/21
二、证伪 “美联储年内加息” 主流叙事，规避长期熊市 🛡️
二.1 当前熊市定义：除AI 外全部资产同步下跌
市场嘲讽 “老登资产”，但全市场早已进入熊市。老登资产覆盖全球工业、金融、互联网、消费、医药、地产、中概、港股、债券、贵金属、加密、石油能源，仅AI 板块独立上涨，其余全线下跌，等同于全面熊市。
AI 长期基本面向好，但当前上涨透支未来估值；若其余板块持续走熊，AI 估值泡沫早晚破裂。当前市场一致押注 2026 美联储加息，支撑逻辑为5 月高通胀、FOMC 鹰派、欧日同步紧缩，下文五大逻辑完整推翻该叙事。
II. Debunking the Consensus Fed Hike Narrative to Avoid a Sustained Broad Bear Market
II.1 Defining the Current Bear Market: Every Asset Slumps Excluding AI
Markets mock boomer assets, yet we are already entrenched in a bear cycle. Originally limited to consumer names, boomer assets now span global industrials, financials, internet software, staples, biotech, real estate, China ADRs, Hong Kong equities, bonds, precious metals, crypto and oil & energy. With only AI holding bullish momentum, every other asset declining qualifies this as a full-scale bear market.
While the AI sector boasts solid long-term fundamentals, its current rally relies on forward valuation speculation and capital rotation. If all other sectors remain mired in bear territory, AI will eventually face a sharp valuation correction as well. The entire market prices heavy odds of a 2026 Fed rate hike, citing May’s hot inflation, hawkish FOMC remarks and synchronized ECB/BoJ tightening. The following five core arguments fully invalidate this mainstream narrative.
🧵  Thread 10/21
二.2 证伪逻辑1：供给端通胀冲击彻底解除，原油进入下行周期
本轮通胀反弹唯一外部诱因是美伊海峡原油封锁，属于一次性供给冲击，并非内需过热带来持续通胀。备忘录落地后海峡航运恢复，OPEC + 闲置产能、美国页岩油完全弥补供给缺口，原油地缘溢价持续消退。
三季度 WTI 中枢回落至70-80 美元，能源成本向化工、运输、零售传导的上行压力消失，6 月起CPI、PPI 能源分项环比转负，从源头切断通胀上行路径。只要不再出现大规模海峡封锁黑天鹅，供给端无持续推升物价动力，美联储无需为一次性扰动加息。
II.2 Debunk Argument 1: Supply-Side Inflation Shock Fully Resolved, Crude Enters Downtrend
The sole external catalyst for this inflation resurgence was strait crude supply disruptions stemming from US-Iran tensions, representing a one-off supply shock rather than persistent inflation driven by overheated domestic demand. Full strait shipping normalization under the US-Iran MOU rapidly closed global crude supply deficits. Spare OPEC+ capacity, US shale output and non-OPEC supply expansions fully offset shortages, gradually erasing crude’s geopolitical risk premium.
WTI’s fair value will slide to $70–80/bbl in Q3, eliminating upward cost pressure passing through chemicals, logistics and retail sectors. Starting June, energy contributions to monthly CPI, PPI and PCE will flip negative, severing the root driver of inflation gains. Barring large-scale strait blockades, no persistent supply-side price pressures remain, removing the Fed’s rationale for hiking rates in response to a temporary shock.

二.3 证伪逻辑2：需求端无滞胀风险，就业与消费韧性充足

市场担忧 “高通胀+ 低增长” 滞胀打压美股，但美国需求端数据无明显疲软。5 月非农新增17.2 万人，失业率稳定 4.3%，短期消费拉动就业，但长期 AI 自动化存在白领失业隐患；零售销售环比 + 0.9% 超预期，剔除能源后居民实际消费稳定，服务业韧性强劲。
温和增长叠加短期能源通胀不属于滞胀，无需激进加息压制总需求，等待油价自然回落即可降温物价，不存在强行加息的经济基础。
II.3 Debunk Argument 2: No Stagflation Risk on Demand Side, Labor & Consumption Remain Resilient
Many investors fear stagflation (high inflation + low growth) will hammer US equities, yet domestic demand data shows no meaningful weakness. May nonfarm payrolls rose by 172,000 with unemployment flat at 4.3%, supported by near-term consumer activity, though AI automation poses long-term white-collar job loss risks. Retail sales climbed 0.9% MoM, beating estimates, while real household consumption excluding energy stayed steady with robust service spending.
The mix of moderate economic growth and temporary energy inflation does not constitute stagflation. Aggressive rate hikes to suppress aggregate demand are unnecessary, as cooling crude prices will naturally ease headline inflation pressures without policy intervention.

When the United States and Iran entered into direct conflict, many analysts predicted that oil would quickly surge to $150 or even $200 per barrel. After all, roughly one-fifth of global oil consumption normally passes through the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most critical energy chokepoints.

Yet, despite the largest disruption in oil supplies in modern history, crude oil mostly remained below $100, briefly peaking around $120 before crashing sharply.

Why didn’t this shock produce the expected spike?

Here are the main reasons:

1. The market expected the disruption to be temporary. Commodity markets price in future expectations, not just current conditions. Investors generally believed that the economic and political costs of a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz would be unsustainable for all parties. As a result, they bet on diplomacy eventually prevailing and were reluctant to price in a permanent supply shock.

2. Not all oil flows stopped. Even during the conflict, a significant portion of Middle Eastern oil continued reaching global markets. Saudi Arabia and the UAE used alternative pipelines that bypass Hormuz, while some shipments continued through informal channels and reorganized logistics. The actual net loss in supply was lower than official figures suggested.

3. Governments released emergency reserves. Strategic Petroleum Reserves were tapped to cushion the shock. These emergency stocks bought valuable time, replaced part of the lost supply, and prevented panic buying in the physical oil markets.

4. Economies are less dependent on oil than in previous decades. Electric vehicles and major efficiency improvements have reduced oil dependency in many economies. The world still needs oil, but each dollar of global GDP now requires significantly less oil than during past energy crises.

5. The market has become far more efficient at handling energy shocks. Since the oil crises of the 1970s, economies have diversified energy sources, built strategic reserves, and strengthened supply chain resilience. This crisis proved that the global economy is simply less vulnerable to oil supply disruptions than it was a few decades ago.

At the beginning of the crisis, I also thought oil could hit $150 or higher. The fact that it didn’t shows how oil has become a less dominant variable for global investors.

If even the temporary closure of the world’s most important oil chokepoint failed to produce a sustained price surge, it becomes difficult to argue for a significant rally in oil prices from here.

Paradoxically, the greater risk now may be to the downside.

Behind the scenes of the recent conflict with Iran, the illusion of a unified Arab Gulf front completely shattered.
​While the public saw a region in crisis, the real drama was happening on high-security phone lines between Abu Dhabi, Riyadh, and Doha. We now know that UAE President Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed (MBZ) aggressively lobbied Saudi Arabia and Qatar to join forces for a coordinated, joint military assault on Iran. He even invoked history, reminding them that the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) was originally founded in 1981 for this exact purpose: mutual defense against Tehran.
​The response he got? A firm, direct refusal. Both neighbors told the UAE, "This is not our war to join."
​The fallout from that rejection has completely redrawn the geopolitical map of the Middle East:
​Saudi Arabia Chose Independence: Instead of getting locked into a UAE-led coalition, Riyadh launched a few calculated, independent strikes of its own before quickly pivoting to back Pakistani-led diplomacy to de-escalate the crisis.
​Qatar Backed Down: Even after Iranian forces hit Qatar’s vital Ras Laffan LNG plant, Doha chose economic self-preservation and regional mediation over military escalation.
​The UAE Was Left Burning—And Furious: Because the UAE bore the absolute brunt of Iran’s retaliation—absorbing nearly 3,000 drones and a relentless barrage of missiles that devastated infrastructure like the Fujairah oil port—Abu Dhabi viewed its neighbors' passivity as a betrayal.
​The UAE was left to mount its own counter-offensives alone, including its high-stakes strike on Iran's Lavan Island.
​This is exactly why we are seeing such radical shifts right now. The UAE's recent, shocking exit from OPEC and its aggressive move to double down on independent military and intelligence ties with Israel aren't random. They are the direct result of a bitter realization: when the chips were down, the "Gulf alliance" existed only on paper. Abu Dhabi is moving forward on its own terms.
